title: trigger...end
html:
trigger...end

trigger...end

Declare a trigger.

trigger TriggerName
    {public soap} ; Execute trigger only
    {public web | partner web }
    {ScopeBlock}
    {ParamsBlock}
    {VariablesBlock}

    ProcScript

    {return {(Value) }}
end

Declaration

  • TriggerName—valid trigger name. For more information, see the "Triggers: System" and "Triggers: Interactive" topics.
  • public web—trigger can be called from a web browser, RESTful service, or other web client when used in DSPs, USPs, and Service components. For more information, see the "web" topic.
  • ScopeBlock—specifies the data to be included in a DSP request-response exchange. For more information, see the "scope...endscope" topic.
  • ParamsBlock—defines the trigger's parameters, if any. For more information, see the "params...endparams" topic.
  • VariablesBlock—defines the local variables used by the trigger, if any. For more information, see the "Variables" topic.

Return Values

Table 1. Values of $procerror commonly returned following trigger
Value Error constant Meaning
-1139 UPROCERR_MODULE_NAME ProcScript module name is not valid

Use

Allowed in all component types.

Description

Use the trigger statement in the Extended Triggers container of the parent object to define an interactive or system trigger.

To mark the end of the trigger definition, use the end statement.

Note: If a webtrigger is defined with the same name as a trigger, the last one defined is the one that is used.
Note: System triggers cannot be declared at the Public Web access level. If you define a system trigger with the public web qualifier, the Uniface component will fail to compile and display the following error: error: 1000 - Public web access is not permitted for system triggers.

Triggers in Web Applications

When defining interactive triggers in DSPs, USPs, and services:

  • Use the public web declaration if you want the trigger to be activated from a web browser or RESTful web service. For example, use this in the exec operation of DSPs and USPs.
  • Use the public soap declaration if you want the trigger to be activated by a SOAP-based client.
  • In DSPs, use the partner web declaration if the trigger is to be referenced by scope definitions of triggers or other operations that are themselves declared as public or partner web.
  • In DSPs, use a scope definition to define the data that will be included in the request-response exchange between the client and the server. If omitted, the scope is assumed to be both input and output, meaning that all data in the DSP will be included in both the request and the response.

Activating triggers from the browser initiates a request-response cycle, since triggers can only be executed on the server. To execute a trigger in the browser, it must be defined with the webtrigger command.

Related Links
  • Triggers: System

    System triggers are script modules that are synchronously executed in response to system events that are initiated by commands. System trigger names are predetermined and case-insensitive.

  • Triggers: Interactive

    Interactive triggers are script modules that are executed in response to events associated with a specific object in a user interface component. They can be fired as a result of user actions, such as clicking a widget or changing its value. Alternatively, some triggers can be invoked by their corresponding macro or the callfieldtrigger ProcScript command.

  • web

    Declares that the trigger or operation can be called from a web client, or that its data can be included in the DSP request-response exchange of other triggers or operations.

  • scope…endscope

    Declares a scope block, which specifies the data that is to be included in a DSP request-response exchange for the current trigger or operation.

  • params…endparams

    Declare the parameter block for an operation, function, or global ProcScript.

  • webtrigger

    Define a trigger in a DSP that will be executed on the client browser.

  • soap

    Declares that the operation can be activated by a SOAP request.

  • Variables

    Variables are placeholders for data whose value can change during the runtime duration of the ProcScript module, component, or application for which they have scope. The scope is determined by where they are declared.

  • Script Modules

    A script module is a named collection of ProcScript belonging to a development object. Script modules are analogous to events and methods in other programming languages.

  • Code Containers

    Code containers are properties of components, application shells, entities and fields that hold the ProcScript and JavaScript code used to define application behavior for the specific object.
